title: Air-fryer roast potatoes
subtitle: Par-boiled, shaken, crisped in oil.
excerpt: Maris Piper potatoes par-boiled until the edges fluff, shaken in the colander to rough the surface, then crisped in the air fryer with a tablespoon of beef dripping or olive oil for thirty minutes. Sunday-roast crunch in half the oven time. Serves four.
sourceNotes: The par-boil-and-shake technique is documented as early as Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), public domain. The air-fryer variant matches the consensus method used by UK air-fryer reviewers; cook times here are for a domestic 5 L drawer.

recipe.freezeNotes: Roast potatoes don't survive the freezer; the texture turns mealy.
recipe.batchNotes: Air-fryer drawer fits about 800 g of potatoes in a single layer. More than that, cook in two waves and keep the first wave warm in a low oven.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Par-boil up to two hours ahead; leave on a tray to dry. The air-fry happens to order.

content.text: Eight hundred grams of Maris Piper or King Edward potatoes peeled and cut into walnut-sized chunks. Par-boiled in salted water for ten minutes, drained, and shaken hard in the colander to rough the surfaces into fluffy edges. Tossed with a tablespoon of duck fat or olive oil, scattered into a pre-heated air-fryer drawer at 200°C, and cooked for thirty minutes with a turn every ten.

The shake is what makes the roast. Smooth boiled potatoes go gold but soft; roughed-edge boiled potatoes catch crisp on every flute and corner. Don't skip the shake. Floury potatoes (Maris Piper, King Edward, Russet) hold the rough edge; waxy potatoes turn into shiny gold disks instead.

items.prepNote: Maris Piper, peeled, cut into walnut-sized chunks
or goose fat, beef dripping, or olive oil
for the boiling water and to finish

content.text: Sit {{potato}} of potato chunks in a large saucepan. Cover with cold water by two finger-widths. Add a generous tablespoon of salt. Bring to the boil. Simmer for 10 minutes; a knife should slide in 1 cm but the centre should still resist.

Drain in a colander. Leave for 30 seconds to steam off the surface water. Then shake the colander hard, three or four times, until the edges of the chunks fluff and rough up. The surfaces should look slightly furred, not smooth.
Set the air fryer to 200°C and pre-heat for three minutes.

Tip the par-boiled potatoes into a mixing bowl. Add {{duck-fat}} of duck fat or oil and toss to coat. Tip into the air-fryer drawer in a single layer with the {{rosemary}} of rosemary leaves and the {{garlic}} of crushed garlic if using.

Cook for 30 minutes at 200°C, opening the drawer at 10 and 20 minutes to shake or turn the potatoes. They should finish deep gold-brown all over, crisp and shattering on the bite. Tip onto a serving dish; scatter with sea salt flakes.

items.cause: No par-boil, or par-boil too short.
items.fix: Boil 10 minutes. Drain, steam-dry, shake. Pre-heat the air fryer before adding.
Cut to walnut size, around 4 cm across. Larger chunks need the par-boil extended to 12 minutes.
One tablespoon for 800 g is the right ratio. The air fryer needs less fat than an oven roast.

items.cause: Drawer not pre-heated; potatoes went on cold metal.
items.fix: Always pre-heat. The hot surface releases the potato as the starch sears.

content.text: Air-fryer roast potatoes are the modern shortcut to the British Sunday-roast standard. They free up the oven for the joint and the trimmings. Serve with anything that wants a roast spud alongside — chicken, beef, pork belly, or a Wednesday-night egg.
